input.prompt:
I'm planning a new fictional "PulseBoard" channel plugin for OpenClaw. I need to understand the plugin SDK contracts and channel types thoroughly.

1. src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts - the channel contract interface
2. src/plugin-sdk/core.ts - core types for plugins
3. src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts - plugin entry point types
4. src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts - plugin channel types
5. src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts - core channel types
6. src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts - adapter types
7. src/plugins/types.ts - plugin discovery types

9. The src/plugin-sdk/ directory listing (all files)

Also look for:
- Any existing test files for channel plugins (*.test.ts) to understand testing patterns
- src/routing/ directory to understand how channels are routed
- Any invariant test files that check plugin manifest structure

Return the full file contents for each file found.

input.prompt:
I'm planning a new fictional "PulseBoard" channel plugin for OpenClaw. I need to understand the plugin manifest format, registry, and invariant tests to ensure the new plugin follows all required conventions.

Please explore and return the FULL contents of:
1. src/plugins/contracts/registry.ts - the plugin contracts registry
2. src/plugins/public-artifacts.ts - public artifacts
3. Any files named *.invariant.test.ts or similar that test plugin structure
4. Look at the workspace configuration (pnpm-workspace.yaml or similar) to understand how bundled plugins are registered
5. Look at the root package.json for workspaces configuration
6. Find and read any AGENTS.md file in the bundled plugin tree root
7. Look for any labeler.yml file (.github/labeler.yml) to understand how plugins are labeled

Also, please look at an existing channel plugin's openclaw.plugin.json manifest files (IRC, Nostr, Telegram, or Matrix) and report ALL fields present, especially:
- id
- openclaw.channel.id
- openclaw.install.npmSpec
- Any channel-specific fields

Return full file contents.

118	/** Options for a plugin entry that registers providers, tools, commands, or services. */
119	type DefinePluginEntryOptions = {
120	  id: string;
121	  name: string;
122	  description: string;
123	  kind?: OpenClawPluginDefinition["kind"];
124	  configSchema?: OpenClawPluginConfigSchema | (() => OpenClawPluginConfigSchema);
125	  register: (api: OpenClawPluginApi) => void;
126	};
127	
128	/** Normalized object shape that OpenClaw loads from a plugin entry module. */
129	type DefinedPluginEntry = {
130	  id: string;
131	  name: string;
132	  description: string;
133	  configSchema: OpenClawPluginConfigSchema;
134	  register: NonNullable<OpenClawPluginDefinition["register"]>;
135	} & Pick<OpenClawPluginDefinition, "kind">;
136	
137	/** Resolve either a concrete config schema or a lazy schema factory. */
138	function resolvePluginConfigSchema(
139	  configSchema: DefinePluginEntryOptions["configSchema"] = emptyPluginConfigSchema,
140	): OpenClawPluginConfigSchema {
141	  return typeof configSchema === "function" ? configSchema() : configSchema;
142	}
143	
144	/**
145	 * Canonical entry helper for non-channel plugins.
146	 *
147	 * Use this for provider, tool, command, service, memory, and context-engine
148	 * plugins. Channel plugins should use `defineChannelPluginEntry(...)` from
149	 * `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core` so they inherit the channel capability wiring.
150	 */
151	export function definePluginEntry({
152	  id,
153	  name,
154	  description,
155	  kind,
156	  configSchema = emptyPluginConfigSchema,
157	  register,
158	}: DefinePluginEntryOptions): DefinedPluginEntry {
159	  return {
160	    id,
161	    name,
162	    description,
163	    ...(kind ? { kind } : {}),
164	    configSchema: resolvePluginConfigSchema(configSchema),
165	    register,
166	  };
167	}

1	# Plugins Boundary
2	
3	This directory owns plugin discovery, manifest validation, loading, registry
4	assembly, and contract enforcement.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/architecture.md`
10	  - `docs/plugins/manifest.md`
11	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`
12	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md`
13	- Definition files:
14	  - `src/plugins/types.ts`
15	  - `src/plugins/runtime/types.ts`
16	  - `src/plugins/contracts/registry.ts`
17	  - `src/plugins/public-artifacts.ts`
18	
19	## Boundary Rules
20	
21	- Preserve manifest-first behavior: discovery, config validation, and setup
22	  should work from metadata before plugin runtime executes.
23	- Keep loader behavior aligned with the documented Plugin SDK and manifest
24	  contracts. Do not create private backdoors that bundled plugins can use but
25	  external plugins cannot.
26	- If a loader or registry change affects plugin authors, update the public SDK,
27	  docs, and contract tests instead of relying on incidental internals.
28	- Do not normalize "plugin-owned" into "core-owned" by scattering direct reads
29	  of `plugins.entries.<id>.config` through unrelated core paths. Prefer generic
30	  helpers, plugin runtime hooks, manifest metadata, and explicit auto-enable
31	  wiring.
32	- When plugin-owned tools or provider fallbacks need core participation, keep
33	  the contract generic and honor plugin disablement plus SecretRef semantics.
34	- Keep contract loading and contract tests on the dedicated bundled registry

1	---
2	summary: "Plugin manifest + JSON schema requirements (strict config validation)"
3	read_when:
4	  - You are building an OpenClaw plugin
5	  - You need to ship a plugin config schema or debug plugin validation errors
6	title: "Plugin Manifest"
7	---
8	
9	# Plugin manifest (openclaw.plugin.json)
10	
11	This page is for the **native OpenClaw plugin manifest** only.
12	
13	For compatible bundle layouts, see [Plugin bundles](/plugins/bundles).
14	
15	Compatible bundle formats use different manifest files:
16	
17	- Codex bundle: `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`
18	- Claude bundle: `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` or the default Claude component
19	  layout without a manifest
20	- Cursor bundle: `.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`
21	
22	OpenClaw auto-detects those bundle layouts too, but they are not validated
23	against the `openclaw.plugin.json` schema described here.
24	
25	For compatible bundles, OpenClaw currently reads bundle metadata plus declared
26	skill roots, Claude command roots, Claude bundle `settings.json` defaults, and
27	supported hook packs when the layout matches OpenClaw runtime expectations.
28	
29	Every native OpenClaw plugin **must** ship a `openclaw.plugin.json` file in the
30	**plugin root**. OpenClaw uses this manifest to validate configuration
31	**without executing plugin code**. Missing or invalid manifests are treated as
32	plugin errors and block config validation.
33	
34	See the full plugin system guide: [Plugins](/tools/plugin).
35	For the native capability model and current external-compatibility guidance:
36	[Capability model](/plugins/architecture#public-capability-model).
37	
38	## What this file does
39	
40	`openclaw.plugin.json` is the metadata OpenClaw reads before it loads your
41	plugin code.
42	
43	Use it for:
44	
45	- plugin identity
46	- config validation
47	- auth and onboarding metadata that should be available without booting plugin
48	  runtime
49	- static capability ownership snapshots used for bundled compat wiring and
50	  contract coverage
51	- config UI hints
52	
53	Do not use it for:
54	
55	- registering runtime behavior
56	- declaring code entrypoints
57	- npm install metadata
58	
59	Those belong in your plugin code and `package.json`.
60	
61	## Minimal example
62	
63	```json
64	{
65	  "id": "voice-call",
66	  "configSchema": {
67	    "type": "object",
68	    "additionalProperties": false,
69	    "properties": {}
70	  }
71	}
72	```
73	
74	## Rich example
75	
76	```json
77	{
78	  "id": "openrouter",
79	  "name": "OpenRouter",
80	  "description": "OpenRouter provider plugin",
81	  "version": "1.0.0",
82	  "providers": ["openrouter"],
83	  "cliBackends": ["openrouter-cli"],
84	  "providerAuthEnvVars": {
85	    "openrouter": ["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"]
86	  },
87	  "providerAuthChoices": [
88	    {
89	      "provider": "openrouter",
90	      "method": "api-key",
91	      "choiceId": "openrouter-api-key",
92	      "choiceLabel": "OpenRouter API key",
93	      "groupId": "openrouter",
94	      "groupLabel": "OpenRouter",
95	      "optionKey": "openrouterApiKey",
96	      "cliFlag": "--openrouter-api-key",
97	      "cliOption": "--openrouter-api-key <key>",
98	      "cliDescription": "OpenRouter API key",
99	      "onboardingScopes": ["text-inference"]
100	    }
101	  ],
102	  "uiHints": {
103	    "apiKey": {
104	      "label": "API key",
105	      "placeholder": "sk-or-v1-...",
106	      "sensitive": true
107	    }
108	  },
109	  "configSchema": {
110	    "type": "object",
111	    "additionalProperties": false,
112	    "properties": {
113	      "apiKey": {
114	        "type": "string"
115	      }
116	    }
117	  }
118	}
119	```
120	
121	## Top-level field reference
122	
123	| Field                 | Required | Type                             | What it means                                                                                                                |
124	| --------------------- | -------- | -------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
125	| `id`                  | Yes      | `string`                         | Canonical plugin id. This is the id used in `plugins.entries.<id>`.                                                          |
126	| `configSchema`        | Yes      | `object`                         | Inline JSON Schema for this plugin's config.                                                                                 |
127	| `enabledByDefault`    | No       | `true`                           | Marks a bundled plugin as enabled by default. Omit it, or set any non-`true` value, to leave the plugin disabled by default. |
128	| `kind`                | No       | `"memory"` \| `"context-engine"` | Declares an exclusive plugin kind used by `plugins.slots.*`.                                                                 |
129	| `channels`            | No       | `string[]`                       | Channel ids owned by this plugin. Used for discovery and config validation.                                                  |
130	| `providers`           | No       | `string[]`                       | Provider ids owned by this plugin.                                                                                           |
131	| `cliBackends`         | No       | `string[]`                       | CLI inference backend ids owned by this plugin. Used for startup auto-activation from explicit config refs.                  |
132	| `providerAuthEnvVars` | No       | `Record<string, string[]>`       | Cheap provider-auth env metadata that OpenClaw can inspect without loading plugin code.                                      |
133	| `providerAuthChoices` | No       | `object[]`                       | Cheap auth-choice metadata for onboarding pickers, preferred-provider resolution, and simple CLI flag wiring.                |
134	| `contracts`           | No       | `object`                         | Static bundled capability snapshot for speech, media-understanding, image-generation, web search, and tool ownership.        |
135	| `skills`              | No       | `string[]`                       | Skill directories to load, relative to the plugin root.                                                                      |
136	| `name`                | No       | `string`                         | Human-readable plugin name.                                                                                                  |
137	| `description`         | No       | `string`                         | Short summary shown in plugin surfaces.                                                                                      |
138	| `version`             | No       | `string`                         | Informational plugin version.                                                                                                |
139	| `uiHints`             | No       | `Record<string, object>`         | UI labels, placeholders, and sensitivity hints for config fields.                                                            |
140	
141	## providerAuthChoices reference
142	
143	Each `providerAuthChoices` entry describes one onboarding or auth choice.
144	OpenClaw reads this before provider runtime loads.
145	
146	| Field              | Required | Type                                            | What it means                                                                                            |
147	| ------------------ | -------- | ----------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
148	| `provider`         | Yes      | `string`                                        | Provider id this choice belongs to.                                                                      |
149	| `method`           | Yes      | `string`                                        | Auth method id to dispatch to.                                                                           |
150	| `choiceId`         | Yes      | `string`                                        | Stable auth-choice id used by onboarding and CLI flows.                                                  |
151	| `choiceLabel`      | No       | `string`                                        | User-facing label. If omitted, OpenClaw falls back to `choiceId`.                                        |
152	| `choiceHint`       | No       | `string`                                        | Short helper text for the picker.                                                                        |
153	| `groupId`          | No       | `string`                                        | Optional group id for grouping related choices.                                                          |
154	| `groupLabel`       | No       | `string`                                        | User-facing label for that group.                                                                        |
155	| `groupHint`        | No       | `string`                                        | Short helper text for the group.                                                                         |
156	| `optionKey`        | No       | `string`                                        | Internal option key for simple one-flag auth flows.                                                      |
157	| `cliFlag`          | No       | `string`                                        | CLI flag name, such as `--openrouter-api-key`.                                                           |
158	| `cliOption`        | No       | `string`                                        | Full CLI option shape, such as `--openrouter-api-key <key>`.                                             |
159	| `cliDescription`   | No       | `string`                                        | Description used in CLI help.                                                                            |
160	| `onboardingScopes` | No       | `Array<"text-inference" \| "image-generation">` | Which onboarding surfaces this choice should appear in. If omitted, it defaults to `["text-inference"]`. |
161	
162	## uiHints reference
163	
164	`uiHints` is a map from config field names to small rendering hints.
165	
166	```json
167	{
168	  "uiHints": {
169	    "apiKey": {
170	      "label": "API key",
171	      "help": "Used for OpenRouter requests",
172	      "placeholder": "sk-or-v1-...",
173	      "sensitive": true
174	    }
175	  }
176	}
177	```
178	
179	Each field hint can include:
180	
181	| Field         | Type       | What it means                           |
182	| ------------- | ---------- | --------------------------------------- |
183	| `label`       | `string`   | User-facing field label.                |
184	| `help`        | `string`   | Short helper text.                      |
185	| `tags`        | `string[]` | Optional UI tags.                       |
186	| `advanced`    | `boolean`  | Marks the field as advanced.            |
187	| `sensitive`   | `boolean`  | Marks the field as secret or sensitive. |
188	| `placeholder` | `string`   | Placeholder text for form inputs.       |
189	
190	## contracts reference
191	
192	Use `contracts` only for static capability ownership metadata that OpenClaw can
193	read without importing the plugin runtime.
194	
195	```json
196	{
197	  "contracts": {
198	    "speechProviders": ["openai"],
199	    "mediaUnderstandingProviders": ["openai", "openai-codex"],
200	    "imageGenerationProviders": ["openai"],
201	    "webSearchProviders": ["gemini"],
202	    "tools": ["firecrawl_search", "firecrawl_scrape"]
203	  }
204	}
205	```
206	
207	Each list is optional:
208	
209	| Field                         | Type       | What it means                                                  |
210	| ----------------------------- | ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
211	| `speechProviders`             | `string[]` | Speech provider ids this plugin owns.                          |
212	| `mediaUnderstandingProviders` | `string[]` | Media-understanding provider ids this plugin owns.             |
213	| `imageGenerationProviders`    | `string[]` | Image-generation provider ids this plugin owns.                |
214	| `webSearchProviders`          | `string[]` | Web-search provider ids this plugin owns.                      |
215	| `tools`                       | `string[]` | Agent tool names this plugin owns for bundled contract checks. |
216	
217	Legacy top-level `speechProviders`, `mediaUnderstandingProviders`, and
218	`imageGenerationProviders` are deprecated. Use `openclaw doctor --fix` to move
219	them under `contracts`; normal manifest loading no longer treats them as
220	capability ownership.
221	
222	## Manifest versus package.json
223	
224	The two files serve different jobs:
225	
226	| File                   | Use it for                                                                                                         |
227	| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
228	| `openclaw.plugin.json` | Discovery, config validation, auth-choice metadata, and UI hints that must exist before plugin code runs           |
229	| `package.json`         | npm metadata, dependency installation, and the `openclaw` block used for entrypoints and setup or catalog metadata |
230	
231	If you are unsure where a piece of metadata belongs, use this rule:
232	
233	- if OpenClaw must know it before loading plugin code, put it in `openclaw.plugin.json`
234	- if it is about packaging, entry files, or npm install behavior, put it in `package.json`
235	
236	## JSON Schema requirements
237	
238	- **Every plugin must ship a JSON Schema**, even if it accepts no config.
239	- An empty schema is acceptable (for example, `{ "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false }`).
240	- Schemas are validated at config read/write time, not at runtime.
241	
242	## Validation behavior
243	
244	- Unknown `channels.*` keys are **errors**, unless the channel id is declared by
245	  a plugin manifest.
246	- `plugins.entries.<id>`, `plugins.allow`, `plugins.deny`, and `plugins.slots.*`
247	  must reference **discoverable** plugin ids. Unknown ids are **errors**.
248	- If a plugin is installed but has a broken or missing manifest or schema,
249	  validation fails and Doctor reports the plugin error.
250	- If plugin config exists but the plugin is **disabled**, the config is kept and
251	  a **warning** is surfaced in Doctor + logs.
252	
253	See [Configuration reference](/gateway/configuration) for the full `plugins.*` schema.
254	
255	## Notes
256	
257	- The manifest is **required for native OpenClaw plugins**, including local filesystem loads.
258	- Runtime still loads the plugin module separately; the manifest is only for
259	  discovery + validation.
260	- Only documented manifest fields are read by the manifest loader. Avoid adding
261	  custom top-level keys here.
262	- `providerAuthEnvVars` is the cheap metadata path for auth probes, env-marker
263	  validation, and similar provider-auth surfaces that should not boot plugin
264	  runtime just to inspect env names.
265	- `providerAuthChoices` is the cheap metadata path for auth-choice pickers,
266	  `--auth-choice` resolution, preferred-provider mapping, and simple onboarding
267	  CLI flag registration before provider runtime loads. For runtime wizard
268	  metadata that requires provider code, see
269	  [Provider runtime hooks](/plugins/architecture#provider-runtime-hooks).
270	- Exclusive plugin kinds are selected through `plugins.slots.*`.
271	  - `kind: "memory"` is selected by `plugins.slots.memory`.
272	  - `kind: "context-engine"` is selected by `plugins.slots.contextEngine`
273	    (default: built-in `legacy`).
274	- `channels`, `providers`, `cliBackends`, and `skills` can be omitted when a
275	  plugin does not need them.
276	- If your plugin depends on native modules, document the build steps and any
277	  package-manager allowlist requirements (for example, pnpm `allow-build-scripts`
278	  - `pnpm rebuild <package>`).
279	
280	## Related
281	
282	- [Building Plugins](/plugins/building-plugins) — getting started with plugins
283	- [Plugin Architecture](/plugins/architecture) — internal architecture
284	- [SDK Overview](/plugins/sdk-overview) — Plugin SDK reference
285

1	# Channels Boundary
2	
3	`src/channels/**` is core channel implementation. Plugin authors should not
4	import from this tree directly.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md`
10	  - `docs/plugins/architecture.md`
11	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`
12	- Definition files:
13	  - `src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts`
14	  - `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts`
15	  - `src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts`
16	  - `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
17	  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts`
18	
19	## Boundary Rules
20	
21	- Keep extension-facing channel surfaces flowing through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`
22	  instead of direct imports from `src/channels/**`.
23	- When a bundled or third-party channel needs a new seam, add a typed SDK
24	  contract or facade first.
25	- Remember that shared channel changes affect both built-in and extension
26	  channels. Check routing, pairing, allowlists, command gating, onboarding, and
27	  reply behavior across the full set.

2	  AllowFromListSchema,
3	  buildChannelConfigSchema,

6	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-primitives";

9	
10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );
27	
28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});
57	
58	export type NostrProfile = z.infer<typeof NostrProfileSchema>;
59	
60	/**
61	 * Zod schema for channels.nostr.* configuration
62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */
77	  privateKey: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */
80	  relays: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
81	
82	  /** DM access policy: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled */
83	  dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional(),
84	
85	  /** Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex format) */
86	  allowFrom: AllowFromListSchema,
87	
88	  /** Profile metadata (NIP-01 kind:0 content) */
89	  profile: NostrProfileSchema.optional(),
90	});

92	export type NostrConfig = z.infer<typeof NostrConfigSchema>;
93	
94	/**

1	# Extensions Boundary
2	
3	This directory contains bundled plugins. Treat it as the same boundary that
4	third-party plugins see.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/building-plugins.md`
10	  - `docs/plugins/architecture.md`
11	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`
12	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md`
13	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md`
14	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md`
15	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md`
16	  - `docs/plugins/manifest.md`
17	- Definition files:
18	  - `src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts`
19	  - `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
20	  - `src/plugin-sdk/provider-entry.ts`
21	  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts`
22	  - `scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json`
23	  - `package.json`
24	
25	## Boundary Rules
26	
27	- Extension production code should import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` and its
28	  own local barrels such as `./api.ts` and `./runtime-api.ts`.
29	- Do not import core internals from `src/**`, `src/channels/**`,
30	  `src/plugin-sdk-internal/**`, or another extension's `src/**`.
31	- Do not use relative imports that escape the current extension package root.
32	- Keep plugin metadata accurate in `openclaw.plugin.json` and the package
33	  `openclaw` block so discovery and setup work without executing plugin code.
34	- Treat files like `src/**`, `onboard.ts`, and other local helpers as private
35	  unless you intentionally promote them through `api.ts` and, if needed, a
36	  matching `src/plugin-sdk/<id>.ts` facade.
37	- If core or core tests need a bundled plugin helper, export it from `api.ts`
38	  first instead of letting them deep-import extension internals.
39	
40	## Expanding The Boundary
41	
42	- If an extension needs a new seam, add a typed Plugin SDK subpath or additive
43	  export instead of reaching into core.
44	- Keep new plugin-facing seams backwards-compatible and versioned. Third-party
45	  plugins consume this surface.
46	- When intentionally expanding the contract, update the docs, exported subpath
47	  list, package exports, and API/contract checks in the same change.
48

1	# @openclaw/nostr
2	
3	Nostr DM channel plugin for OpenClaw using NIP-04 encrypted direct messages.
4	
5	## Overview
6	
7	This extension adds Nostr as a messaging channel to OpenClaw. It enables your bot to:
8	
9	- Receive encrypted DMs from Nostr users
10	- Send encrypted responses back
11	- Work with any NIP-04 compatible Nostr client (Damus, Amethyst, etc.)
12	
13	## Installation
14	
15	```bash
16	openclaw plugins install @openclaw/nostr
17	```
18	
19	## Quick Setup
20	
21	1. Generate a Nostr keypair (if you don't have one):
22	
23	   ```bash
24	   # Using nak CLI
25	   nak key generate
26	
27	   # Or use any Nostr key generator
28	   ```
29	
30	2. Add to your config:
31	
32	   ```json
33	   {
34	     "channels": {
35	       "nostr": {
36	         "privateKey": "${NOSTR_PRIVATE_KEY}",
37	         "relays": ["wss://relay.damus.io", "wss://nos.lol"]
38	       }
39	     }
40	   }
41	   ```
42	
43	3. Set the environment variable:
44	
45	   ```bash
46	   export NOSTR_PRIVATE_KEY="nsec1..."  # or hex format
47	   ```
48	
49	4. Restart the gateway
50	
51	## Configuration
52	
53	| Key          | Type     | Default                                     | Description                                                |
54	| ------------ | -------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
55	| `privateKey` | string   | required                                    | Bot's private key (nsec or hex format)                     |
56	| `relays`     | string[] | `["wss://relay.damus.io", "wss://nos.lol"]` | WebSocket relay URLs                                       |
57	| `dmPolicy`   | string   | `"pairing"`                                 | Access control: `pairing`, `allowlist`, `open`, `disabled` |
58	| `allowFrom`  | string[] | `[]`                                        | Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex)                       |
59	| `enabled`    | boolean  | `true`                                      | Enable/disable the channel                                 |
60	| `name`       | string   | -                                           | Display name for the account                               |
61	
62	## Access Control
63	
64	### DM Policies
65	
66	- **pairing** (default): Unknown senders receive a pairing code to request access
67	- **allowlist**: Only pubkeys in `allowFrom` can message the bot
68	- **open**: Anyone can message the bot (use with caution)
69	- **disabled**: DMs are disabled
70	
71	Inbound event signatures are verified before policy enforcement and NIP-04 decryption.
72	Unknown senders in `pairing` mode can receive a pairing reply, but their original DM body is not
73	processed unless approved.
74	
75	### Example: Allowlist Mode
76	
77	```json
78	{
79	  "channels": {
80	    "nostr": {
81	      "privateKey": "${NOSTR_PRIVATE_KEY}",
82	      "dmPolicy": "allowlist",
83	      "allowFrom": ["npub1abc...", "0123456789abcdef..."]
84	    }
85	  }
86	}
87	```
88	
89	## Testing
90	
91	### Local Relay (Recommended)
92	
93	```bash
94	# Using strfry
95	docker run -p 7777:7777 ghcr.io/hoytech/strfry
96	
97	# Configure openclaw to use local relay
98	"relays": ["ws://localhost:7777"]
99	```
100	
101	### Manual Test
102	
103	1. Start the gateway with Nostr configured
104	2. Open Damus, Amethyst, or another Nostr client
105	3. Send a DM to your bot's npub
106	4. Verify the bot responds
107	
108	## Protocol Support
109	
110	| NIP    | Status    | Notes                  |
111	| ------ | --------- | ---------------------- |
112	| NIP-01 | Supported | Basic event structure  |
113	| NIP-04 | Supported | Encrypted DMs (kind:4) |
114	| NIP-17 | Planned   | Gift-wrapped DMs (v2)  |
115	
116	## Security Notes
117	
118	- Private keys are never logged
119	- Event signatures are verified before processing
120	- Sender policy is checked before expensive crypto work
121	- Inbound DMs are rate-limited and oversized payloads are dropped before decrypt
122	- Use environment variables for keys, never commit to config files
123	- Consider using `allowlist` mode in production
124	
125	## Troubleshooting
126	
127	### Bot not receiving messages
128	
129	1. Verify private key is correctly configured
130	2. Check relay connectivity
131	3. Ensure `enabled` is not set to `false`
132	4. Check the bot's public key matches what you're sending to
133	
134	### Messages not being delivered
135	
136	1. Check relay URLs are correct (must use `wss://`)
137	2. Verify relays are online and accepting connections
138	3. Check for rate limiting (reduce message frequency)
139	
140	## License
141	
142	MIT
143

1	---
2	summary: "Plugin internals: capability model, ownership, contracts, load pipeline, and runtime helpers"
3	read_when:
4	  - Building or debugging native OpenClaw plugins
5	  - Understanding the plugin capability model or ownership boundaries
6	  - Working on the plugin load pipeline or registry
7	  - Implementing provider runtime hooks or channel plugins
8	title: "Plugin Internals"
9	sidebarTitle: "Internals"
10	---
11	
12	# Plugin Internals
13	
14	<Info>
15	  This is the **deep architecture reference**. For practical guides, see:
16	  - [Install and use plugins](/tools/plugin) — user guide
17	  - [Getting Started](/plugins/building-plugins) — first plugin tutorial
18	  - [Channel Plugins](/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins) — build a messaging channel
19	  - [Provider Plugins](/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins) — build a model provider
20	  - [SDK Overview](/plugins/sdk-overview) — import map and registration API
21	</Info>
22	
23	This page covers the internal architecture of the OpenClaw plugin system.
24	
25	## Public capability model
26	
27	Capabilities are the public **native plugin** model inside OpenClaw. Every
28	native OpenClaw plugin registers against one or more capability types:
29	
30	| Capability            | Registration method                           | Example plugins           |
31	| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
32	| Text inference        | `api.registerProvider(...)`                   | `openai`, `anthropic`     |
33	| CLI inference backend | `api.registerCliBackend(...)`                 | `openai`, `anthropic`     |
34	| Speech                | `api.registerSpeechProvider(...)`             | `elevenlabs`, `microsoft` |
35	| Media understanding   | `api.registerMediaUnderstandingProvider(...)` | `openai`, `google`        |
36	| Image generation      | `api.registerImageGenerationProvider(...)`    | `openai`, `google`        |
37	| Web search            | `api.registerWebSearchProvider(...)`          | `google`                  |
38	| Channel / messaging   | `api.registerChannel(...)`                    | `msteams`, `matrix`       |
39	
40	A plugin that registers zero capabilities but provides hooks, tools, or
41	services is a **legacy hook-only** plugin. That pattern is still fully supported.
42	
43	### External compatibility stance
44	
45	The capability model is landed in core and used by bundled/native plugins
46	today, but external plugin compatibility still needs a tighter bar than "it is
47	exported, therefore it is frozen."
48	
49	Current guidance:
50	
51	- **existing external plugins:** keep hook-based integrations working; treat
52	  this as the compatibility baseline
53	- **new bundled/native plugins:** prefer explicit capability registration over
54	  vendor-specific reach-ins or new hook-only designs
55	- **external plugins adopting capability registration:** allowed, but treat the
56	  capability-specific helper surfaces as evolving unless docs explicitly mark a
57	  contract as stable
58	
59	Practical rule:
60	
61	- capability registration APIs are the intended direction
62	- legacy hooks remain the safest no-breakage path for external plugins during
63	  the transition
64	- exported helper subpaths are not all equal; prefer the narrow documented
65	  contract, not incidental helper exports
66	
67	### Plugin shapes
68	
69	OpenClaw classifies every loaded plugin into a shape based on its actual
70	registration behavior (not just static metadata):
71	
72	- **plain-capability** -- registers exactly one capability type (for example a
73	  provider-only plugin like `mistral`)
74	- **hybrid-capability** -- registers multiple capability types (for example
75	  `openai` owns text inference, speech, media understanding, and image
76	  generation)
77	- **hook-only** -- registers only hooks (typed or custom), no capabilities,
78	  tools, commands, or services
79	- **non-capability** -- registers tools, commands, services, or routes but no
80	  capabilities
81	
82	Use `openclaw plugins inspect <id>` to see a plugin's shape and capability
83	breakdown. See [CLI reference](/cli/plugins#inspect) for details.
84	
85	### Legacy hooks
86	
87	The `before_agent_start` hook remains supported as a compatibility path for
88	hook-only plugins. Legacy real-world plugins still depend on it.
89	
90	Direction:
91	
92	- keep it working
93	- document it as legacy
94	- prefer `before_model_resolve` for model/provider override work
95	- prefer `before_prompt_build` for prompt mutation work
96	- remove only after real usage drops and fixture coverage proves migration safety
97	
98	### Compatibility signals
99	
100	When you run `openclaw doctor` or `openclaw plugins inspect <id>`, you may see
101	one of these labels:
102	
103	| Signal                     | Meaning                                                      |
104	| -------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
105	| **config valid**           | Config parses fine and plugins resolve                       |
106	| **compatibility advisory** | Plugin uses a supported-but-older pattern (e.g. `hook-only`) |
107	| **legacy warning**         | Plugin uses `before_agent_start`, which is deprecated        |
108	| **hard error**             | Config is invalid or plugin failed to load                   |
109	
110	Neither `hook-only` nor `before_agent_start` will break your plugin today --
111	`hook-only` is advisory, and `before_agent_start` only triggers a warning. These
112	signals also appear in `openclaw status --all` and `openclaw plugins doctor`.
113	
114	## Architecture overview
115	
116	OpenClaw's plugin system has four layers:
117	
118	1. **Manifest + discovery**
119	   OpenClaw finds candidate plugins from configured paths, workspace roots,
120	   global extension roots, and bundled extensions. Discovery reads native
121	   `openclaw.plugin.json` manifests plus supported bundle manifests first.
122	2. **Enablement + validation**
123	   Core decides whether a discovered plugin is enabled, disabled, blocked, or
124	   selected for an exclusive slot such as memory.
125	3. **Runtime loading**
126	   Native OpenClaw plugins are loaded in-process via jiti and register
127	   capabilities into a central registry. Compatible bundles are normalized into
128	   registry records without importing runtime code.
129	4. **Surface consumption**
130	   The rest of OpenClaw reads the registry to expose tools, channels, provider
131	   setup, hooks, HTTP routes, CLI commands, and services.
132	
133	For plugin CLI specifically, root command discovery is split in two phases:
134	
135	- parse-time metadata comes from `registerCli(..., { descriptors: [...] })`
136	- the real plugin CLI module can stay lazy and register on first invocation
137	
138	That keeps plugin-owned CLI code inside the plugin while still letting OpenClaw
139	reserve root command names before parsing.
140	
141	The important design boundary:
142	
143	- discovery + config validation should work from **manifest/schema metadata**
144	  without executing plugin code
145	- native runtime behavior comes from the plugin module's `register(api)` path
146	
147	That split lets OpenClaw validate config, explain missing/disabled plugins, and
148	build UI/schema hints before the full runtime is active.
149	
150	### Channel plugins and the shared message tool
151	
152	Channel plugins do not need to register a separate send/edit/react tool for
153	normal chat actions. OpenClaw keeps one shared `message` tool in core, and
154	channel plugins own the channel-specific discovery and execution behind it.
155	
156	The current boundary is:
157	
158	- core owns the shared `message` tool host, prompt wiring, session/thread
159	  bookkeeping, and execution dispatch
160	- channel plugins own scoped action discovery, capability discovery, and any
161	  channel-specific schema fragments
162	- channel plugins own provider-specific session conversation grammar, such as
163	  how conversation ids encode thread ids or inherit from parent conversations
164	- channel plugins execute the final action through their action adapter
165	
166	For channel plugins, the SDK surface is
167	`ChannelMessageActionAdapter.describeMessageTool(...)`. That unified discovery
168	call lets a plugin return its visible actions, capabilities, and schema
169	contributions together so those pieces do not drift apart.
170	
171	Core passes runtime scope into that discovery step. Important fields include:
172	
173	- `accountId`
174	- `currentChannelId`
175	- `currentThreadTs`
176	- `currentMessageId`
177	- `sessionKey`
178	- `sessionId`
179	- `agentId`
180	- trusted inbound `requesterSenderId`
181	
182	That matters for context-sensitive plugins. A channel can hide or expose
183	message actions based on the active account, current room/thread/message, or
184	trusted requester identity without hardcoding channel-specific branches in the
185	core `message` tool.
186	
187	This is why embedded-runner routing changes are still plugin work: the runner is
188	responsible for forwarding the current chat/session identity into the plugin
189	discovery boundary so the shared `message` tool exposes the right channel-owned
190	surface for the current turn.
191	
192	For channel-owned execution helpers, bundled plugins should keep the execution
193	runtime inside their own extension modules. Core no longer owns the Discord,
194	Slack, Telegram, or WhatsApp message-action runtimes under `src/agents/tools`.
195	We do not publish separate `plugin-sdk/*-action-runtime` subpaths, and bundled
196	plugins should import their own local runtime code directly from their
197	extension-owned modules.
198

1 --- 2 title: "Building Channel Plugins" 3 sidebarTitle: "Channel Plugins" 4 summary: "Step-by-step guide to building a messaging channel plugin for OpenClaw" 5 read_when: 6 - You are building a new messaging channel plugin 7 - You want to connect OpenClaw to a messaging platform 8 - You need to understand the ChannelPlugin adapter surface 9 --- 10 11 # Building Channel Plugins 12 13 This guide walks through building a channel plugin that connects OpenClaw to a 14 messaging platform. By the end you will have a working channel with DM security, 15 pairing, reply threading, and outbound messaging. 16 17 18 If you have not built any OpenClaw plugin before, read 19 [Getting Started](/plugins/building-plugins) first for the basic package 20 structure and manifest setup. 21 22 23 ## How channel plugins work 24 25 Channel plugins do not need their own send/edit/react tools. OpenClaw keeps one 26 shared `message` tool in core. Your plugin owns: 27 28 - **Config** — account resolution and setup wizard 29 - **Security** — DM policy and allowlists 30 - **Pairing** — DM approval flow 31 - **Session grammar** — how provider-specific conversation ids map to base chats, thread ids, and parent fallbacks 32 - **Outbound** — sending text, media, and polls to the platform 33 - **Threading** — how replies are threaded 34 35 Core owns the shared message tool, prompt wiring, the outer session-key shape, 36 generic `:thread:` bookkeeping, and dispatch. 37 38 If your platform stores extra scope inside conversation ids, keep that parsing 39 in the plugin with `messaging.resolveSessionConversation(...)`. That is the 40 canonical hook for mapping `rawId` to the base conversation id, optional thread 41 id, explicit `baseConversationId`, and any `parentConversationCandidates`. 42 When you return `parentConversationCandidates`, keep them ordered from the 43 narrowest parent to the broadest/base conversation. 44 45 Bundled plugins that need the same parsing before the channel registry boots 46 can also expose a top-level `session-key-api.ts` file with a matching 47 `resolveSessionConversation(...)` export. Core uses that bootstrap-safe surface 48 only when the runtime plugin registry is not available yet. 49 50 `messaging.resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` remains available as a 51 legacy compatibility fallback when a plugin only needs parent fallbacks on top 52 of the generic/raw id. If both hooks exist, core uses 53 `resolveSessionConversation(...).parentConversationCandidates` first and only 54 falls back to `resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` when the canonical hook 55 omits them. 56 57 ## Approvals and channel capabilities 58 59 Most channel plugins do not need approval-specific code. 60 61 - Core owns same-chat `/approve`, shared approval button payloads, and generic fallback delivery. 62 - Prefer one `approvalCapability` object on the channel plugin when the channel needs approval-specific behavior. 63 - `approvalCapability.authorizeActorAction` and `approvalCapability.getActionAvailabilityState` are the canonical approval-auth seam. 64 - Use `outbound.shouldSuppressLocalPayloadPrompt` or `outbound.beforeDeliverPayload` for channel-specific payload lifecycle behavior such as hiding duplicate local approval prompts or sending typing indicators before delivery. 65 - Use `approvalCapability.delivery` only for native approval routing or fallback suppression. 66 - Use `approvalCapability.render` only when a channel truly needs custom approval payloads instead of the shared renderer. 67 - If a channel can infer stable owner-like DM identities from existing config, use `createResolvedApproverActionAuthAdapter` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` to restrict same-chat `/approve` without adding approval-specific core logic. 68 - If a channel needs native approval delivery, keep channel code focused on target normalization and transport hooks. Use `createChannelExecApprovalProfile`, `createChannelNativeOriginTargetResolver`, `createChannelApproverDmTargetResolver`, `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalCapability`, and `createChannelNativeApprovalRuntime` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` so core owns request filtering, routing, dedupe, expiry, and gateway subscription. 69 - Native approval channels must route both `accountId` and `approvalKind` through those helpers. `accountId` keeps multi-account approval policy scoped to the right bot account, and `approvalKind` keeps exec vs plugin approval behavior available to the channel without hardcoded branches in core. 70 - `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalAdapter` still exists as a compatibility wrapper, but new code should prefer the capability builder and expose `approvalCapability` on the plugin. 71 72 Auth-only channels can usually stop at the default path: core handles approvals and the plugin just exposes outbound/auth capabilities. Native approval channels such as Matrix, Slack, Telegram, and custom chat transports should use the shared native helpers instead of rolling their own approval lifecycle. 73 74 ## Walkthrough 75 76 77 78 79 Create the standard plugin files. The `channel` field in `package.json` is 80 what makes this a channel plugin: 81 82 83 ```json package.json 84 { 85 "name": "@myorg/openclaw-acme-chat", 86 "version": "1.0.0", 87 "type": "module", 88 "openclaw": { 89 "extensions": ["./index.ts"], 90 "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts", 91 "channel": { 92 "id": "acme-chat", 93 "label": "Acme Chat", 94 "blurb": "Connect OpenClaw to Acme Chat." 95 } 96 } 97 } 98 ``` 99 100 ```json openclaw.plugin.json 101 { 102 "id": "acme-chat", 103 "kind": "channel", 104 "channels": ["acme-chat"], 105 "name": "Acme Chat", 106 "description": "Acme Chat channel plugin", 107 "configSchema": { 108 "type": "object", 109 "additionalProperties": false, 110 "properties": { 111 "acme-chat": { 112 "type": "object", 113 "properties": { 114 "token": { "type": "string" }, 115 "allowFrom": { 116 "type": "array", 117 "items": { "type": "string" } 118 } 119 } 120 } 121 } 122 } 123 } 124 ``` 125 126 127 128 129 130 The `ChannelPlugin` interface has many optional adapter surfaces. Start with 131 the minimum — `id` and `setup` — and add adapters as you need them. 132 133 Create `src/channel.ts`: 134 135 ```typescript src/channel.ts 136 import { 137 createChatChannelPlugin, 138 createChannelPluginBase, 139 } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 140 import type { OpenClawConfig } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 141 import { acmeChatApi } from "./client.js"; // your platform API client 142 143 type ResolvedAccount = { 144 accountId: string | null; 145 token: string; 146 allowFrom: string[]; 147 dmPolicy: string | undefined; 148 }; 149 150 function resolveAccount( 151 cfg: OpenClawConfig, 152 accountId?: string | null, 153 ): ResolvedAccount { 154 const section = (cfg.channels as Record)?.["acme-chat"]; 155 const token = section?.token; 156 if (!token) throw new Error("acme-chat: token is required"); 157 return { 158 accountId: accountId ?? null, 159 token, 160 allowFrom: section?.allowFrom ?? [], 161 dmPolicy: section?.dmSecurity, 162 }; 163 } 164 165 export const acmeChatPlugin = createChatChannelPlugin({ 166 base: createChannelPluginBase({ 167 id: "acme-chat", 168 setup: { 169 resolveAccount, 170 inspectAccount(cfg, accountId) { 171 const section = 172 (cfg.channels as Record)?.["acme-chat"]; 173 return { 174 enabled: Boolean(section?.token), 175 configured: Boolean(section?.token), 176 tokenStatus: section?.token ? "available" : "missing", 177 }; 178 }, 179 }, 180 }), 181 182 // DM security: who can message the bot 183 security: { 184 dm: { 185 channelKey: "acme-chat", 186 resolvePolicy: (account) => account.dmPolicy, 187 resolveAllowFrom: (account) => account.allowFrom, 188 defaultPolicy: "allowlist", 189 }, 190 }, 191 192 // Pairing: approval flow for new DM contacts 193 pairing: { 194 text: { 195 idLabel: "Acme Chat username", 196 message: "Send this code to verify your identity:", 197 notify: async ({ target, code }) => { 198 await acmeChatApi.sendDm(target, `Pairing code: ${code}`); 199 }, 200 }, 201 }, 202 203 // Threading: how replies are delivered 204 threading: { topLevelReplyToMode: "reply" }, 205 206 // Outbound: send messages to the platform 207 outbound: { 208 attachedResults: { 209 sendText: async (params) => { 210 const result = await acmeChatApi.sendMessage( 211 params.to, 212 params.text, 213 ); 214 return { messageId: result.id }; 215 }, 216 }, 217 base: { 218 sendMedia: async (params) => { 219 await acmeChatApi.sendFile(params.to, params.filePath); 220 }, 221 }, 222 }, 223 }); 224 ``` 225 226 227 Instead of implementing low-level adapter interfaces manually, you pass 228 declarative options and the builder composes them: 229 230 | Option | What it wires | 231 | --- | --- | 232 | `security.dm` | Scoped DM security resolver from config fields | 233 | `pairing.text` | Text-based DM pairing flow with code exchange | 234 | `threading` | Reply-to-mode resolver (fixed, account-scoped, or custom) | 235 | `outbound.attachedResults` | Send functions that return result metadata (message IDs) | 236 237 You can also pass raw adapter objects instead of the declarative options 238 if you need full control. 239 240 241 242 243 244 Create `index.ts`: 245 246 ```typescript index.ts 247 import { defineChannelPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; content.text:
Perfect! I now have comprehensive information about the OpenClaw plugin system. Let me create a detailed summary document for you.

## OpenClaw Plugin Architecture: Complete Reference

I've thoroughly explored the OpenClaw plugin system and gathered all requested documentation. Here is the complete reference for planning your "PulseBoard" channel plugin:

---

### 1. **src/plugins/contracts/registry.ts** - FULL CONTENTS

**File Path:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467461413-32ajrf/src/plugins/contracts/registry.ts`

This file contains the plugin contract registry with these key components:

```typescript
// Type definitions for contract entries:
- CapabilityContractEntry<T>: pluginId + provider mapping
- ProviderContractEntry: maps plugins to their providers
- WebSearchProviderContractEntry: includes credentialValue
- WebFetchProviderContractEntry: includes credentialValue
- SpeechProviderContractEntry: speech providers
- MediaUnderstandingProviderContractEntry: media providers
- ImageGenerationProviderContractEntry: image generation
- PluginRegistrationContractEntry: consolidated plugin registration

// Key exports:
- providerContractRegistry: lazy array of all provider contracts
- uniqueProviderContractProviders: deduped providers
- providerContractPluginIds: all provider plugin IDs
- webSearchProviderContractRegistry: web search contracts
- webFetchProviderContractRegistry: web fetch contracts
- speechProviderContractRegistry: speech provider contracts
- mediaUnderstandingProviderContractRegistry: media understanding contracts
- imageGenerationProviderContractRegistry: image generation contracts
- pluginRegistrationContractRegistry: consolidated plugin registration

// Key functions:
- requireProviderContractProvider(providerId): lookup provider by ID
- resolveProviderContractPluginIdsForProvider(): find plugins providing a capability
- resolveProviderContractProvidersForPluginIds(): lookup providers for plugins
- resolveWebSearchProviderContractEntriesForPluginId(): web search contracts
- resolveWebFetchProviderContractEntriesForPluginId(): web fetch contracts
```

Uses lazy loading with `createLazyArrayView()` to minimize startup impact.

---

### 2. **src/plugins/public-artifacts.ts** - FULL CONTENTS

**File Path:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467461413-32ajrf/src/plugins/public-artifacts.ts`

```typescript
// Core exports:
export function getPublicArtifactBasename(relativePath: string): string
  // Extracts filename from path like "dist/extensions/irc/index.js"

export const BUNDLED_RUNTIME_SIDECAR_PATHS: string[]
  // All runtime sidecar artifact paths from bundled plugins
  // Example: "dist/extensions/discord/runtime-api.js"

export const BUNDLED_RUNTIME_SIDECAR_BASENAMES: string[]
  // Unique basenames of sidecar artifacts

export const GUARDED_EXTENSION_PUBLIC_SURFACE_BASENAMES: string[]
  // Protected public surface files like:
  // - "action-runtime.runtime.js"
  // - "api.js"
  // - "channel-config-api.js"
  // - "runtime-api.js"
  // - "setup-entry.js"
  // - "session-key-api.js"
```

These constants guard against unintended public surface exposure in plugins.

---

### 3. **Invariant Tests** - CONTRACT TEST FILES

**No `*.invariant.test.ts` files exist**, but there are comprehensive **contract tests**:

**Key Contract Test Files:**
- `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-registration.contract.test.ts` - validates plugin registration metadata
- `src/plugins/contracts/discovery.contract.test.ts` - validates plugin discovery
- `src/plugins/contracts/package-manifest.contract.test.ts` - validates package.json manifest structure
- `src/plugins/bundle-manifest.test.ts` - validates bundle manifest parsing (Codex, Claude, Cursor)
- `src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts` - validates bundled plugin metadata collection
- `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` - validates channel plugin ID resolution

**Channel Plugin Invariants (implicit):**
- Each channel plugin must have `channels: [<channel-id>]` in manifest
- Must define `configSchema` (empty object acceptable)
- Channel ID must be discoverable (in `plugins.entries.<id>`)
- Setup must resolve accounts and inspect configuration state

---

### 4. **pnpm-workspace.yaml** - FULL CONTENTS

**File Path:** `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775467461413-32ajrf/pnpm-workspace.yaml`

```yaml
packages:
  - .                          # Root package
  - ui                         # UI workspace
  - packages/*                 # Package workspaces (plugin-sdk, etc.)
  - extensions/*               # All bundled plugin extensions

minimumReleaseAge: 2880        # Minimum 2 days before release

# Exclude these patterns from version checking
minimumReleaseAgeExclude:
  - "openclaw"
  - "@mariozechner/*"
  - "@typescript/native-preview*"
  - "@oxlint/*"
  - "@oxfmt/*"
  - "sqlite-vec*"

# Native modules that require platform-specific builds
onlyBuiltDependencies:
  - "@lydell/node-pty"
  - "@matrix-org/matrix-sdk-crypto-nodejs"
  - "@napi-rs/canvas"
  - "@whiskeysockets/baileys"
  - "sharp"
  - ... (others)

# Built dependencies to ignore
ignoredBuiltDependencies:
  - "@discordjs/opus"

# Plugins Boundary

This directory owns:
- Plugin discovery
- Manifest validation
- Loading
- Registry assembly
- Contract enforcement

## Public Contracts

**Docs:**
- `docs/plugins/architecture.md` - internals, capability model, contracts
- `docs/plugins/manifest.md` - plugin manifest (openclaw.plugin.json)
- `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` - SDK overview
- `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md` - entry point contracts

**Definition files:**
- `src/plugins/types.ts` - core plugin types
- `src/plugins/runtime/types.ts` - runtime types
- `src/plugins/contracts/registry.ts` - contract registry
- `src/plugins/public-artifacts.ts` - public artifact definitions

## Boundary Rules

1. **Manifest-first:** Discovery, config validation, setup must work from 
   metadata before plugin runtime executes.

2. **Keep loader aligned:** Do not create private backdoors that bundled 
   plugins use but external plugins cannot.

3. **Update docs when changing contracts:** If loader/registry changes affect 
   plugin authors, update SDK docs and contract tests.

4. **Avoid plugin-config scatter:** Use generic helpers, plugin runtime hooks, 
   and manifest metadata instead of direct config reads.

5. **Keep contracts on bundled registry path:** Contract loading and tests 
   belong on dedicated bundled registry, not production resolution flows.

6. **Honor plugin disablement:** When plugin-owned tools need core participation, 
   keep contracts generic and honor plugin disablement + SecretRef semantics.
```

- Must pass contract tests

This file contains the plugin contract registry with these key components:

```typescript
// Type definitions for contract entries:
- CapabilityContractEntry<T>: pluginId + provider mapping
- ProviderContractEntry: maps plugins to their providers
- WebSearchProviderContractEntry: includes credentialValue
- WebFetchProviderContractEntry: includes credentialValue
- SpeechProviderContractEntry: speech providers
- MediaUnderstandingProviderContractEntry: media providers
- ImageGenerationProviderContractEntry: image generation
- PluginRegistrationContractEntry: consolidated plugin registration

// Key exports:
- providerContractRegistry: lazy array of all provider contracts
- uniqueProviderContractProviders: deduped providers
- providerContractPluginIds: all provider plugin IDs
- webSearchProviderContractRegistry: web search contracts
- webFetchProviderContractRegistry: web fetch contracts
- speechProviderContractRegistry: speech provider contracts
- mediaUnderstandingProviderContractRegistry: media understanding contracts
- imageGenerationProviderContractRegistry: image generation contracts
- pluginRegistrationContractRegistry: consolidated plugin registration

// Key functions:
- requireProviderContractProvider(providerId): lookup provider by ID
- resolveProviderContractPluginIdsForProvider(): find plugins providing a capability
- resolveProviderContractProvidersForPluginIds(): lookup providers for plugins
- resolveWebSearchProviderContractEntriesForPluginId(): web search contracts
- resolveWebFetchProviderContractEntriesForPluginId(): web fetch contracts
```

Uses lazy loading with `createLazyArrayView()` to minimize startup impact.

### 9. **Channel Plugin package.json** - EXTENDED EXAMPLE

#### **Discord Plugin (Most Complete)**
**File:** `extensions/discord/package.json`

```json
{
  "name": "@openclaw/discord",
  "version": "2026.4.1-beta.1",
  "description": "OpenClaw Discord channel plugin",
  "type": "module",
  "dependencies": {
    "@buape/carbon": "0.0.0-beta-20260327000044",
    "@discordjs/voice": "^0.19.2",
    "discord-api-types": "^0.38.43",
    "https-proxy-agent": "^8.0.0",
    "opusscript": "^0.0.8"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "openclaw": "workspace:*"
  },
  "peerDependencies": {
    "openclaw": ">=2026.4.1-beta.1"
  },
  "peerDependenciesMeta": {
    "openclaw": {
      "optional": true
    }
  },
  "openclaw": {
    "extensions": ["./index.ts"],
    "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts",
    "channel": {
      "id": "discord",
      "label": "Discord",
      "selectionLabel": "Discord (Bot API)",
      "detailLabel": "Discord Bot",
      "docsPath": "/channels/discord",
      "docsLabel": "discord",
      "blurb": "very well supported right now.",
      "systemImage": "bubble.left.and.bubble.right",
      "markdownCapable": true
    },
    "install": {
      "npmSpec": "@openclaw/discord",
      "defaultChoice": "npm",
      "minHostVersion": ">=2026.4.1"
    },
    "bundle": {
      "stageRuntimeDependencies": true
    },
    "release": {
      "publishToNpm": true
    }
  }
}
```

**All Fields Explained:**
- **`name`**: NPM package name (scoped to `@openclaw/`)
- **`type: "module"`**: ES module support
- **`dependencies`**: Platform SDK requirements
- **`peerDependencies`**: OpenClaw version requirements
- **`openclaw.extensions`**: Entry point files to load
- **`openclaw.setupEntry`**: Setup/onboarding wizard entry
- **`openclaw.channel.id`**: Channel identifier (used in config)
- **`openclaw.channel.label`**: Display name
- **`openclaw.channel.selectionLabel`**: UI picker label
- **`openclaw.channel.detailLabel`**: Account detail label
- **`openclaw.channel.docsPath`**: Link to channel docs
- **`openclaw.channel.blurb`**: Support status description
- **`openclaw.channel.systemImage`**: SF Symbol identifier
- **`openclaw.channel.markdownCapable`**: Markdown support flag
- **`openclaw.install.npmSpec`**: NPM installation spec
- **`openclaw.install.defaultChoice`**: Default install method
- **`openclaw.install.minHostVersion`**: Min OpenClaw version
- **`openclaw.bundle.stageRuntimeDependencies`**: Bundle runtime deps
- **`openclaw.release.publishToNpm`**: Publish to NPM registry

### 10. **src/channels/AGENTS.md** - Channel Plugin Boundary - FULL CONTENTS

```markdown
# Channels Boundary

`src/channels/**` is core channel implementation. 
Plugin authors should NOT import from this tree directly.

## Public Contracts

**Docs:**
- `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md` - channel plugin building guide
- `docs/plugins/architecture.md` - plugin architecture
- `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` - SDK overview

**Definition files:**
- `src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts` - channel plugin types
- `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` - core channel types
- `src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts` - adapter interfaces
- `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts` - channel registration API
- `src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts` - channel contract

## Boundary Rules

1. **Use plugin-sdk exports:** Keep extension-facing channel surfaces flowing 
   through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` instead of direct imports from `src/channels/**`.

2. **Add SDK seams first:** When a bundled or third-party channel needs a new 
   seam, add a typed SDK contract or facade first.

3. **Check full channel impact:** Remember that shared channel changes affect 
   both built-in and extension channels. Check:
   - routing
   - pairing
   - allowlists
   - command gating
   - onboarding
   - reply behavior
```

---

### 11. **Plugin Manifest (openclaw.plugin.json) - FULL SPECIFICATION**

**File Path:** `docs/plugins/manifest.md`

**Channel Plugin Minimal Example:**
```json
{
  "id": "pulseboard",
  "channels": ["pulseboard"],
  "configSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "additionalProperties": false,
    "properties": {}
  }
}
```

**Channel Plugin Rich Example:**
```json
{
  "id": "pulseboard",
  "name": "PulseBoard",
  "description": "PulseBoard messaging channel plugin",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "channels": ["pulseboard"],
  "configSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "additionalProperties": false,
    "properties": {
      "pulseboard": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "token": { "type": "string" },
          "apiUrl": { "type": "string" },
          "allowFrom": {
            "type": "array",
            "items": { "type": "string" }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "uiHints": {
    "token": {
      "label": "API Token",
      "placeholder": "pb_...",
      "sensitive": true
    },
    "apiUrl": {
      "label": "API URL",
      "placeholder": "https://api.pulseboard.com"
    }
  }
}
```

**All Manifest Fields:**

| Field | Required | Type | Purpose |
|-------|----------|------|---------|
| `id` | Yes | `string` | Plugin ID (used in `plugins.entries.<id>`) |
| `configSchema` | Yes | `object` | JSON Schema for config validation |
| `channels` | For channels | `string[]` | Channel IDs owned by this plugin |
| `name` | No | `string` | Display name |
| `description` | No | `string` | Short description |
| `version` | No | `string` | Plugin version |
| `uiHints` | No | `Record<string, object>` | UI labels & hints |
| `enabledByDefault` | No | `boolean` | Auto-enable for bundled plugins |
| `providers` | No | `string[]` | Provider IDs (for provider plugins) |
| `cliBackends` | No | `string[]` | CLI backend IDs |
| `providerAuthEnvVars` | No | `Record<string, string[]>` | Auth env metadata |
| `providerAuthChoices` | No | `object[]` | Auth choice metadata |
| `contracts` | No | `object` | Static capability ownership |
| `skills` | No | `string[]` | Skill directories to load |

---

### 12. **Key Contract Tests for Channel Plugins**

**File:** `src/plugins/contracts/package-manifest.contract.test.ts`

Channel plugins tested include:
- `bluebubbles`
- `discord`
- `googlechat`
- `irc`
- `line`
- `matrix`
- `mattermost`
- `msteams`
- `nextcloud-talk`
- `nostr`
- `signal`
- `slack`
- `telegram`
- `tlon`
- `twitch`
- `voice-call`
- `whatsapp`
- `zalo`
- `zalouser`

**What's tested:**
- `minHostVersionBaseline` - minimum OpenClaw version
- `runtimeDeps` - critical runtime dependencies
- Package manifest structure validation
- Setup entry detection
- Config schema validation

---

### 13. **Key Test Patterns for Plugin Validation**

**src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts** shows:

Channel plugins must:
1. Be discoverable via manifest registry
2. Have `channels: [<id>]` declared
3. Be included in startup when configured
4. Support `plugins.entries.<id>` references
5. Validate against `plugins.allow` and `plugins.deny` allowlists
6. Include account setup/inspection capabilities

---

### 14. **SDK Channel Plugin Building Guide**

**From docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md:**

**Minimum Required:**
1. `package.json` with `openclaw.channel` block
2. `openclaw.plugin.json` manifest with `channels: [...]`
3. `index.ts` entry point with `defineChannelPluginEntry()`
4. `setup-entry.ts` for account setup/onboarding
5. Implement `ChannelPlugin` interface with:
   - `id`: Channel identifier
   - `setup`: Account resolution and inspection
   - `security`: DM allowlists and policies
   - `pairing`: DM pairing/verification flow
   - `threading`: Reply mode configuration
   - `outbound`: Send text/media implementations

**Optional Adapters:**
- `approvalCapability`: Approval/action authorization
- `messaging`: Session conversation resolution
- Custom handlers for specific actions

---

## Summary for PulseBoard Plugin Design

**Required Files:**
```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── package.json                 # With openclaw.channel block
├── openclaw.plugin.json        # Manifest with channels: ["pulseboard"]
├── index.ts                    # Entry point (defineChannelPluginEntry)
├── setup-entry.ts              # Setup wizard
├── src/
│   └── channel.ts              # ChannelPlugin implementation
└── src/client.ts               # PulseBoard API client
```

**Registry Requirements:**
- Plugin ID: `"pulseboard"`
- Channel ID: `"pulseboard"`
- Must be in `extensions/` workspace
- Needs entry in `.github/labeler.yml`
- Must pass contract tests

**Testing Requirements:**
- `src/plugins/contracts/package-manifest.contract.test.ts` - add to test suite
- Validate manifest structure
- Verify setup capabilities
- Test config schema validation

All documentation, contracts, and test patterns are above. Your PulseBoard plugin should follow the same structure as Discord, Slack, Telegram, Matrix, IRC, or Nostr examples provided.

1	# @openclaw/nostr
2	
3	Nostr DM channel plugin for OpenClaw using NIP-04 encrypted direct messages.
4	
5	## Overview
6	
7	This extension adds Nostr as a messaging channel to OpenClaw. It enables your bot to:
8	
9	- Receive encrypted DMs from Nostr users
10	- Send encrypted responses back
11	- Work with any NIP-04 compatible Nostr client (Damus, Amethyst, etc.)
12	
13	## Installation
14	
15	```bash
16	openclaw plugins install @openclaw/nostr
17	```
18	
19	## Quick Setup
20	
21	1. Generate a Nostr keypair (if you don't have one):
22	
23	   ```bash
24	   # Using nak CLI
25	   nak key generate
26	
27	   # Or use any Nostr key generator
28	   ```
29	
30	2. Add to your config:
31	
32	   ```json
33	   {
34	     "channels": {
35	       "nostr": {
36	         "privateKey": "${NOSTR_PRIVATE_KEY}",
37	         "relays": ["wss://relay.damus.io", "wss://nos.lol"]
38	       }
39	     }
40	   }
41	   ```
42	
43	3. Set the environment variable:
44	
45	   ```bash
46	   export NOSTR_PRIVATE_KEY="nsec1..."  # or hex format
47	   ```
48	
49	4. Restart the gateway
50	
51	## Configuration
52	
53	| Key          | Type     | Default                                     | Description                                                |
54	| ------------ | -------- | ------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
55	| `privateKey` | string   | required                                    | Bot's private key (nsec or hex format)                     |
56	| `relays`     | string[] | `["wss://relay.damus.io", "wss://nos.lol"]` | WebSocket relay URLs                                       |
57	| `dmPolicy`   | string   | `"pairing"`                                 | Access control: `pairing`, `allowlist`, `open`, `disabled` |
58	| `allowFrom`  | string[] | `[]`                                        | Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex)                       |
59	| `enabled`    | boolean  | `true`                                      | Enable/disable the channel                                 |
60	| `name`       | string   | -                                           | Display name for the account                               |
61	
62	## Access Control
63	
64	### DM Policies
65	
66	- **pairing** (default): Unknown senders receive a pairing code to request access
67	- **allowlist**: Only pubkeys in `allowFrom` can message the bot
68	- **open**: Anyone can message the bot (use with caution)
69	- **disabled**: DMs are disabled
70	
71	Inbound event signatures are verified before policy enforcement and NIP-04 decryption.
72	Unknown senders in `pairing` mode can receive a pairing reply, but their original DM body is not
73	processed unless approved.
74	
75	### Example: Allowlist Mode
76	
77	```json
78	{
79	  "channels": {
80	    "nostr": {
81	      "privateKey": "${NOSTR_PRIVATE_KEY}",
82	      "dmPolicy": "allowlist",
83	      "allowFrom": ["npub1abc...", "0123456789abcdef..."]
84	    }
85	  }
86	}
87	```
88	
89	## Testing
90	
91	### Local Relay (Recommended)
92	
93	```bash
94	# Using strfry
95	docker run -p 7777:7777 ghcr.io/hoytech/strfry
96	
97	# Configure openclaw to use local relay
98	"relays": ["ws://localhost:7777"]
99	```
100	
101	### Manual Test
102	
103	1. Start the gateway with Nostr configured
104	2. Open Damus, Amethyst, or another Nostr client
105	3. Send a DM to your bot's npub
106	4. Verify the bot responds
107	
108	## Protocol Support
109	
110	| NIP    | Status    | Notes                  |
111	| ------ | --------- | ---------------------- |
112	| NIP-01 | Supported | Basic event structure  |
113	| NIP-04 | Supported | Encrypted DMs (kind:4) |
114	| NIP-17 | Planned   | Gift-wrapped DMs (v2)  |
115	
116	## Security Notes
117	
118	- Private keys are never logged
119	- Event signatures are verified before processing
120	- Sender policy is checked before expensive crypto work
121	- Inbound DMs are rate-limited and oversized payloads are dropped before decrypt
122	- Use environment variables for keys, never commit to config files
123	- Consider using `allowlist` mode in production
124	
125	## Troubleshooting
126	
127	### Bot not receiving messages
128	
129	1. Verify private key is correctly configured
130	2. Check relay connectivity
131	3. Ensure `enabled` is not set to `false`
132	4. Check the bot's public key matches what you're sending to
133	
134	### Messages not being delivered
135	
136	1. Check relay URLs are correct (must use `wss://`)
137	2. Verify relays are online and accepting connections
138	3. Check for rate limiting (reduce message frequency)
139	
140	## License
141	
142	MIT
143

117	## 2026.1.19-1
118	
119	Initial release.
120	
121	### Features
122	
123	- NIP-04 encrypted DM support (kind:4 events)
124	- Key validation (hex and nsec formats)
125	- Multi-relay support with sequential fallback
126	- Event signature verification
127	- TTL-based deduplication (24h)
128	- Access control via dmPolicy (pairing, allowlist, open, disabled)
129	- Pubkey normalization (hex/npub)
130	
131	### Protocol Support
132	
133	- NIP-01: Basic event structure
134	- NIP-04: Encrypted direct messages
135	
136	### Planned for v2
137	
138	- NIP-17: Gift-wrapped DMs
139	- NIP-44: Versioned encryption
140	- Media attachments
141

1	import {
2	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
3	  BlockStreamingCoalesceSchema,
4	  DmConfigSchema,
5	  DmPolicySchema,
6	  GroupPolicySchema,
7	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
8	  ReplyRuntimeConfigSchemaShape,
9	  ToolPolicySchema,
10	  requireOpenAllowFrom,
11	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-schema";
12	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
13	import { ircChannelConfigUiHints } from "./config-ui-hints.js";
14	
15	const IrcGroupSchema = z
16	  .object({
17	    requireMention: z.boolean().optional(),
18	    tools: ToolPolicySchema,
19	    toolsBySender: z.record(z.string(), ToolPolicySchema).optional(),
20	    skills: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
21	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
22	    allowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
23	    systemPrompt: z.string().optional(),
24	  })
25	  .strict();
26	
27	const IrcNickServSchema = z
28	  .object({
29	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
30	    service: z.string().optional(),
31	    password: z.string().optional(),
32	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
33	    register: z.boolean().optional(),
34	    registerEmail: z.string().optional(),
35	  })
36	  .strict()
37	  .superRefine((value, ctx) => {
38	    if (value.register && !value.registerEmail?.trim()) {
39	      ctx.addIssue({
40	        code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
41	        path: ["registerEmail"],
42	        message: "channels.irc.nickserv.register=true requires channels.irc.nickserv.registerEmail",
43	      });
44	    }
45	  });
46	
47	export const IrcAccountSchemaBase = z
48	  .object({
49	    name: z.string().optional(),
50	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
51	    dangerouslyAllowNameMatching: z.boolean().optional(),
52	    host: z.string().optional(),
53	    port: z.number().int().min(1).max(65535).optional(),
54	    tls: z.boolean().optional(),
55	    nick: z.string().optional(),
56	    username: z.string().optional(),
57	    realname: z.string().optional(),
58	    password: z.string().optional(),
59	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
60	    nickserv: IrcNickServSchema.optional(),
61	    dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional().default("pairing"),
62	    allowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
63	    groupPolicy: GroupPolicySchema.optional().default("allowlist"),
64	    groupAllowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
65	    groups: z.record(z.string(), IrcGroupSchema.optional()).optional(),
66	    channels: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
67	    mentionPatterns: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
68	    markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
69	    ...ReplyRuntimeConfigSchemaShape,
70	  })
71	  .strict();
72	
73	export const IrcAccountSchema = IrcAccountSchemaBase.superRefine((value, ctx) => {
74	  requireOpenAllowFrom({
75	    policy: value.dmPolicy,
76	    allowFrom: value.allowFrom,
77	    ctx,
78	    path: ["allowFrom"],
79	    message: 'channels.irc.dmPolicy="open" requires channels.irc.allowFrom to include "*"',
80	  });
81	});
82	
83	export const IrcConfigSchema = IrcAccountSchemaBase.extend({
84	  accounts: z.record(z.string(), IrcAccountSchema.optional()).optional(),
85	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
86	}).superRefine((value, ctx) => {
87	  requireOpenAllowFrom({
88	    policy: value.dmPolicy,
89	    allowFrom: value.allowFrom,
90	    ctx,
91	    path: ["allowFrom"],
92	    message: 'channels.irc.dmPolicy="open" requires channels.irc.allowFrom to include "*"',
93	  });
94	});
95	
96	export const IrcChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(IrcConfigSchema, {
97	  uiHints: ircChannelConfigUiHints,
98	});

1	import {
2	  AllowFromListSchema,
3	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
4	  DmPolicySchema,
5	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
6	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-primitives";
7	import { buildSecretInputSchema } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input";
8	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
9	
10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );
27	
28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});
57	
58	export type NostrProfile = z.infer<typeof NostrProfileSchema>;
59	
60	/**
61	 * Zod schema for channels.nostr.* configuration
62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */
77	  privateKey: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */
80	  relays: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
81	
82	  /** DM access policy: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled */
